title: Deadlifts as Honest Anger Work
dek: *You've been told anger needs to be managed, controlled, or channeled into something productive. But what if the strongest thing you could do with rage is simply let it complete its arc through your nervous system while your hands are wrapped around a barbell? Here's what happens when you stop performing calm and start moving weight.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Deadlifts turn suppressed anger into measurable weight you can see, lift, and set down with intention. The movement pattern mirrors the physical shape of rage held in your body and gives it permission to move through muscle. This is not metaphor work - it's somatic completion of an interrupted fight response.

You've been sold the idea that anger is something to transcend. That's the first lie. The second is that your body doesn't remember every swallowed sentence, every time you smiled through contempt, every moment you made yourself small to keep the peace. Your hip flexors remember. Your jaw remembers. The space between your shoulder blades remembers. I'm going to tell you something that sounds too simple to work: picking up heavy things and setting them down again is one of the most honest conversations you can have with your own rage. Not because lifting is aggressive. Because it's true. ## The Shape of Suppressed Force Stand in front of a loaded barbell. Before you touch it, notice what your body is already doing. Are your fists clenched? Is your breath shallow and high in your chest? Are you grinding your molars? These aren't separate from your anger. They are your anger, frozen mid-expression. Peter Levine's work on trauma and the body shows us that incomplete defensive responses - the fight, flight, or freeze reactions that never got to finish - live on as held tension and dysregulation. When you were taught that anger was dangerous or ugly or unwelcome, your nervous system learned to interrupt the impulse before it could complete. The energy didn't vanish. It locked into muscle tissue, waiting. A deadlift asks your body to do exactly what anger wants to do: generate massive force from the ground up, engage every major muscle group simultaneously, and move something immovable. The setup position itself - hips back, chest proud, hands gripping something real - is the physical posture of readiness your body has been trying to assume for years. In my own practice, I've watched people cry during their first heavy pull. Not from pain. From recognition. ## Why This Movement and Not Others You could run. You could punch a bag. You could do burpees until you collapse. All of these have their place, and we've written about many of them across the articles we've published on this site. But deadlifts offer something structurally different. The movement has a clear beginning, middle, and end. You approach the bar with intention. You set your position. You create full-body tension. You lift. You stand. You reverse the motion with control. You release. This arc mirrors the healthy expression of anger: acknowledge, build, express, complete, release. Compare that to running, where the motion is repetitive and continuous, or to a heavy bag, where the endpoint is your own exhaustion rather than the completion of a single defined task. Deadlifts teach your nervous system that force can be summoned, used, and then consciously put down. That you can be powerful and then be done being powerful. The barbell doesn't care why you're angry. It doesn't ask you to justify your feelings or explain whether your response is proportionate. It just sits there, offering you honest resistance. You either move it or you don't. There's no performance, no audience to manage. Just you and the weight and the truth of whether you can lift it right now. This is the opposite of how anger usually works in your life, where you're constantly calculating how much is safe to show, who can handle it, whether you have the right to feel this way at all. ## The Nervous System Wants Completion, Not Catharsis Here's what doesn't work: screaming into a pillow and calling it "release." Hitting things while imagining the face of someone who wronged you. Trying to "get it all out" in one explosive session. Harriet Lerner writes about the difference between venting anger and using it as information. Venting often just rehashes the same neural pathways, rehearsing your grievance without changing your relationship to it. The anger you scream out today will be back tomorrow, same shape, same intensity, because you haven't given your body a way to complete the stress cycle. What the nervous system needs is not catharsis but completion. It needs to know that the threat has been met with adequate response, that you generated the force required, that you are now safe to return to baseline. A deadlift provides that arc. The weight is the resistance. Your body's response is proportional, full, and real. When you set the bar down, your nervous system receives the signal: task complete. Threat addressed. Stand down. Over the years I've seen people transform their relationship with anger not by talking about it more skillfully but by giving their bodies a repetitive, reliable way to finish what their nervous system started years ago. The sixth rep of a heavy set teaches something no amount of journaling can: that you can meet resistance, apply yourself fully, and then rest. ## Setting Up: The Ritual Matters Before you touch the weight, you need to arrive in your body. This isn't a warm-up in the conventional sense. It's an acknowledgment. Stand two feet from the bar. Close your eyes. Scan from your feet up through your calves, knees, hips, low back, mid-back, shoulders, neck, jaw. Where are you holding? Where is the grip? Name it quietly: "I'm clenching my jaw. My right hip is tight. My shoulders are up by my ears." Now approach the bar. Your feet should be hip-width, the bar over the middle of your foot. When you hinge to grip it, you're not collapsing. You're folding at the hips with your spine long, your chest open. Your hands wrap the bar just outside your shins. This setup position is not incidental. You are lowering yourself to meet the weight on its terms. You are making yourself structurally sound before you ask your body to produce force. These are not metaphors. These are biomechanical requirements that happen to teach emotional skills. Before you pull, you create tension. You drag the bar toward your shins without lifting it. You pull the slack out of your arms. You breathe into your belly. You feel your lats engage, your hamstrings load. You are gathering. This is what anger looks like when it's not suppressed or exploded: gathered, organized, ready. ## The Pull Itself: Permission to Use Your Whole Body You push the floor away. That's the cue that changes everything. You're not hauling the bar up with your back. You're driving your feet into the ground and letting the earth push back. Your quads fire. Your glutes clench. Your hamstrings and spinal erectors work as a unit. Your lats pull the bar into your body. Your traps stabilize. Your core braces. Your grip holds. This is全身 force. Not one muscle group doing the work while the others stay polite. Not your upper body pretending to be strong while your legs stay decorative. Every part of you is required, recruited, alive. When the bar clears your knees, your hips drive forward. You stand tall. For one breath, you hold the weight at lockout. You are upright, loaded, and steady. Feel that. You are holding something heavy and you are not collapsing. You are not apologizing for the space you take up. You are not dimming yourself to make the weight comfortable. Then you reverse. You push your hips back, you control the descent, you set the bar down with intention. Not a drop. Not a collapse. A controlled return. Each rep is a full cycle: gather, express, complete, release. The weight you use matters less than the quality of attention you bring. Three reps at a weight that demands your full presence will teach you more than fifteen reps you can complete while thinking about groceries. ## What Happens in the Body During the Lift Your heart rate spikes. Your breath quickens. Adrenaline and noradrenaline flood your system. Your muscles generate force. Your nervous system reads this as: I am meeting the threat. I am adequate to this moment. Then you set the bar down. Your heart rate begins to drop. Your breath deepens. The neurochemical wash shifts. Your parasympathetic nervous system gets the signal that the activation was successful and is now complete. This is the same cycle your body wanted to complete every time you swallowed your anger. The same preparation, the same mobilization, the same resolution. But this time, nothing was interrupted. The loop closed. According to the APA's research summary on anger management, the most effective interventions for chronic anger help people recognize their physiological arousal early and develop strategies to complete the stress response rather than suppress it. Deadlifts are not listed in their protocols, but the mechanism fits perfectly: you're training your body to notice activation, meet it fully, and return to baseline. If you struggle with sleep or intrusive thoughts after heavy lifting sessions, you might consider supporting your recovery with something like [NOW L-Tryptophan 500 mg (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0019LWV56?tag=spankyspinola-20) or [Solgar GABA 500 mg (100 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0001OP014?tag=spankyspinola-20), both of which can help your nervous system downregulate after intense physical work. ## The Difference Between Aggressive Lifting and Angry Lifting There's a genre of fitness culture that glorifies rage as fuel. "Use your anger!" "Channel your pain!" "Get angry and lift heavy!" That's not what I'm describing. Aggressive lifting is performative. It's anger as theater, anger as motivation, anger whipped into a frenzy to override your body's signals about what it can actually handle. It leads to injury, to ego-driven loads, to the same suppression in a new costume. Angry lifting - honest angry lifting - is quiet. It's meeting the emotion where it lives in your tissue and giving it a clear task. It doesn't require yelling or face-slapping or ammonia or a hype playlist. It requires presence. You don't need to get angry to lift. You're already angry. The anger is already in you, woven through your fascia, locked in your psoas. The lift just gives it a shape it can finally complete. Thich Nhat Hanh taught that anger is like a crying baby. You don't shake the baby. You don't ignore the baby. You pick the baby up and hold it with care. The deadlift is how you hold your anger with care - not by soothing it away, but by giving it your full, respectful attention and a safe place to be exactly what it is. ## A Five-Minute Practice You Can Do Right Now You don't need a barbell to begin this conversation with your body. Find a heavy object: a full laundry basket, a case of water bottles, a loaded backpack, a bag of dog food. Something that weighs at least twenty pounds and has handles or edges you can grip. Set it on the floor in front of you. Stand with your feet hip-width apart, the object between your feet. Take three full breaths. Notice what you're feeling. Don't edit it. Hinge at your hips - push your butt back, keep your chest proud, let your hands reach for the object. Grip it firmly. Before you lift, create tension. Pull the object toward you slightly without lifting it off the ground. Feel your whole body organize. Breathe into your belly. Now push the floor away with your feet and stand up, bringing the object with you. Stand tall for one full breath. Then reverse: hips back, chest still proud, set the object down with control. Do this three times. Slowly. With your full attention on what your body is doing, what it's feeling, what it wants to say. That's the practice. Not a workout. A listening. If you want to go deeper with the emotional framework around this kind of physical work, Beverly Engel's [Honor Your Anger](https://www.amazon.com/dp/0806540788?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a compassionate, practical guide to anger as information rather than pathology. ## When the Weight Becomes a Mirror In our experience, people don't stay at the same weight for long once they start deadlifting with this kind of attention. Not because they're chasing numbers, but because the weight that felt impossible last month becomes the weight that feels honest this month. Your body is reorganizing. The tension you've been holding in defense is becoming tension you can create and release on purpose. The difference is enormous. You'll know you're doing this right when you finish a set and feel more settled, not more activated. When your breath deepens instead of scatters. When you can put the weight down and walk away without needing to immediately do something else to discharge. The weight becomes a mirror. It shows you what you're actually capable of, which is always more than what you've been allowed to believe about yourself. It shows you the difference between force and violence, between power and aggression, between intensity and harm. You can be strong without being mean. You can take up space without being selfish. You can use your full capacity without apology. The barbell won't gaslight you. It won't tell you you're overreacting. It won't suggest you're too sensitive or that you should calm down. It will just sit there, offering you the exact same challenge it offered yesterday, and you will either be ready for it or you won't. And both of those outcomes are information. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Can I do this if I've never lifted weights before? Yes. Start with a weight that feels manageable but not trivial - a loaded backpack, a heavy bag, even a broom handle to learn the movement pattern. The goal is not to lift heavy immediately. The goal is to learn the shape of gathering your force, expressing it, and completing the cycle. You can find beginner-friendly progressions in [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit), which includes bodyweight variations of this same pattern. If you eventually move to a barbell, consider working with a coach for a few sessions to ensure your form protects your spine. ### What if I feel sadness instead of anger when I lift? That's common and completely valid. Anger and grief are close neighbors. Sometimes what you've been calling anger is actually heartbreak that wasn't safe to feel. Sometimes the rage is covering a deeper sorrow. Let whatever comes up be there. The deadlift doesn't demand that you feel one specific thing. It just asks that you feel it fully and let your body complete the movement. Tears during a heavy set are not a sign you're doing it wrong. They're a sign something true is moving. ### How often should I do this kind of lifting? Two to three times per week is a sustainable rhythm for most people. This gives your nervous system time to integrate what happened, your muscles time to recover, and your emotional body time to process. This is not a daily practice. It's too metabolically demanding and too emotionally intense. Treat it the way you'd treat any other deep work: with respect for the recovery it requires. On the days between, you might explore other practices we discuss in [more articles in this journal](/articles), including breathwork, walking, or simply resting. ## What the Weight Knows The barbell has been teaching people things about themselves for a long time. Longer than any of us have been alive. It doesn't have a philosophy. It doesn't have a method. It just has mass and the immutable fact that you either generate enough force to move it or you don't. This is clarifying in a world that tells you your feelings are negotiable, your boundaries are selfish, your anger is unbecoming. The weight doesn't care what you were taught. It doesn't care what you're supposed to be. It only responds to what you actually do. There's a strange freedom in that. A strange relief. You can learn more about the broader approach to anger as information and practice on [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), where we explore the full range of emotional literacy tools. Or you can start here, in your body, with something heavy in your hands and the simple question: what happens if I let myself be as strong as I actually am? The anger isn't going anywhere. It's been with you for years, maybe decades, shaping how you move through the world. But it doesn't have to stay locked. It can move. It can complete. It can become something you do on purpose instead of something that happens to you. Pick up the weight. Set it down. Notice what changes. That's the work. Not the metaphor of the work. The work itself, measured in pounds and breath and the slow, honest thaw of a nervous system that's finally allowed to finish what it started. You'll find your way. We've seen it happen enough times to trust the process. Start where you are. Use what you have. Let the weight teach you what words never could. If you want to understand more about why we approach anger this way, [the about page](/about) offers context on the philosophy behind this practice. But you don't need to understand it fully before you begin. You just need to be willing to pick something up. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on deadlifts as honest anger work, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
